title: Top Singular Value by Power Method
constraints: Initialize v as the all-ones vector of length n and normalize it.
For each iteration, compute u = A @ v and normalize u.
Then compute v = A.T @ u and normalize v.
After all iterations, return ||A @ v||_2 as the approximate top singular value.
If a zero norm appears, return 0.0.
Do not call np.linalg.svd or np.linalg.eig.
statement:
Write a Python function `top_singular_value_power_method(matrix, num_iters)` that approximates the largest singular value using alternating power iterations.

Instead of computing all singular values, a large-scale algorithm often only estimates the dominant singular direction. The update alternates between the right and left singular vector directions:

$$
u^{(t)} = \frac{A v^{(t)}}{\|A v^{(t)}\|_2}, \qquad v^{(t+1)} = \frac{A^T u^{(t)}}{\|A^T u^{(t)}\|_2}.
$$

After the iterations, estimate the top singular value by

$$
\sigma_1 \approx \|A v\|_2.
$$

Use the deterministic initialization described in the constraints.
reference_solution:
import numpy as np

def top_singular_value_power_method(matrix, num_iters):
    A = np.asarray(matrix, dtype=float)
    n = A.shape[1]
    v = np.ones(n, dtype=float)
    v = v / np.linalg.norm(v)
    for _ in range(num_iters):
        u = A @ v
        u_norm = np.linalg.norm(u)
        if u_norm == 0:
            return 0.0
        u = u / u_norm
        v = A.T @ u
        v_norm = np.linalg.norm(v)
        if v_norm == 0:
            return 0.0
        v = v / v_norm
    return float(np.linalg.norm(A @ v))
solution_explanation: The method repeatedly applies A and A.T to amplify the dominant singular directions. The final norm ||A v|| estimates the largest singular value.
